mission.title: Install and Configure Strimzi Kafka Operator
mission.description: Strimzi Kafka Operator allows you to run Apache Kafka clusters on Kubernetes or OpenShift. It simplifies the deployment and management of Kafka by providing a Kubernetes-native way to handle Kafka resources. Key trade-offs include the need for Kubernetes expertise and the complexity of Kafka configurations.

steps.title: Add the Strimzi Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Strimzi Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add strimzi https://strimzi.io/charts/

```

> This is the official repository for Strimzi.

steps.title: Install the Strimzi Kafka Operator
steps.description:
This command installs the Strimzi Kafka Operator in the 'kafka' namespace using the specified chart version.

```bash

kubectl create namespace kafka

helm install strimzi strimzi/strimzi-kafka-operator --namespace kafka --version 0.44.0

```

> Create a namespace for the Kafka operator.
> Install the Strimzi Kafka Operator.

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Strimzi Kafka Operator is running correctly in the Kubernetes cluster.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kafka

kubectl get deployment strimzi-cluster-operator -n kafka

```

> This will list all pods in the kafka namespace.
> Verify the deployment status of the Kafka operator.

steps.title: Create a Kafka cluster
steps.description:
This step creates a Kafka cluster using a custom resource definition (CRD).

```bash

cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: kafka.strimzi.io/v1beta2
kind: Kafka
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: kafka
spec:
  kafka:
    version: "3.1.0"
    replicas: 3
    listeners:
      plain:
        authentication:
          type: tls
      tls:
        authentication:
          type: tls
    storage:
      type: persistent-claim
      size: 5Gi
  zookeeper:
    replicas: 3
    storage:
      type: persistent-claim
      size: 5Gi
EOF

```

> This YAML creates a Kafka cluster named 'my-cluster'.
commands:

    replicas: 3

      type: persistent-claim
      size: 5Gi
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Kafka cluster
steps.description:
Check the status of the Kafka cluster to ensure it is running as expected.

```bash

kubectl get kafka -n kafka

kubectl describe kafka my-cluster -n kafka

```

> This will show the status of the Kafka cluster.
> Get detailed information about the Kafka cluster.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the Strimzi Kafka Operator
uninstall.description:
This will remove the Strimzi Kafka Operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this may lead to data loss if the Kafka cluster is not backed up.

```bash

helm uninstall strimzi --namespace kafka

kubectl delete namespace kafka

```

> Uninstall the Strimzi Kafka Operator.
> Delete the Kafka namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Strimzi Kafka Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your Kafka data. This process will upgrade the operator and verify its functionality post-upgrade.

```bash

kubectl get kafka -n kafka -o yaml > kafka-backup.yaml

helm upgrade strimzi strimzi/strimzi-kafka-operator --namespace kafka --version 0.44.0

kubectl get pods -n kafka

```

> Backup the current Kafka configuration.
> Upgrade the Strimzi Kafka Operator.
> Verify that all pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get kafka -n kafka -o yaml > kafka-backup.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unable to connect to the Kafka broker'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the Kafka broker is not running or if there are network issues.

**Fix:**
Check the status of the Kafka pods with 'kubectl get pods -n kafka' and ensure they are running.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Kafka cluster not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Kafka cluster CRD has not been created or is not recognized.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the Kafka cluster CRD is applied correctly using 'kubectl apply -f <your-kafka-crd-file.yaml>'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient resources to create Kafka broker'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may happen if the Kubernetes nodes do not have enough resources allocated.

**Fix:**
Check the resource limits of your nodes and adjust the Kafka cluster specifications accordingly.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to pull image'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can occur if the specified Kafka image is not available or if there are network issues.

**Fix:**
Verify the image name and tag in your Kafka CRD and ensure your cluster has internet access.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Kafka 3.1.0, improved metrics collection, and enhanced RBAC configurations.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated support for Kafka versions below 2.8.0.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 0.43, ensure to update your Kafka CRDs to match the new specifications.

resolution.summary: You should see the Kafka cluster status as 'Ready' when running 'kubectl get kafka -n kafka'.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with sufficient resources.
